title: Team Structure, Incentives, Pre-Construction Pricing, and Cash Flow
summary: This open workshop covered a wide range of operational and business questions from participants, including how to structure and scale pre-construction and estimating teams, how to design employee bonus and incentive programs, and how to price pre-construction services. Participants also discussed cash flow management, spec versus custom home building models, and strategies for managing client budget expectations throughout the design-build process.

teachings.concept: Owner as Last Line of Defense in Estimating
teachings.explanation: No matter how well a team is built, the owner's eyes and ears are sharper when it comes to catching pricing errors because they feel the financial pain of a wrong number. Fully delegating estimating without owner oversight is risky, especially on smaller teams.
teachings.quote: As we're owners, our eyes and our ears are way more keen than someone who may not feel the pain of a role number. So like, that's the one thing I have learned.

teachings.concept: Agility Over Rigid Role Definitions on Small Teams
teachings.explanation: Small construction teams must remain flexible, with team members covering multiple functions as needed. Strict role separation is a luxury of larger organizations; smaller teams thrive by being willing to shift responsibilities based on current demands.
teachings.quote: In this business that changes all the time. We're like, we're always covering for each other. In fact, on our next project, we're pulling the project manager in to price it.

teachings.explanation: Incentivizing employees individually by project profit can create competition and animosity among team members. A team-wide profit-sharing model encourages collaboration and mutual support across projects.
teachings.quote: For us, it's all about the team operating. Like I want project managers calling other project managers for help. So that the whole team is elevated, rather than individual, you know, like my project don't bother me.

teachings.explanation: Rather than tying bonuses solely to on-time and on-budget delivery (which is simply doing the job), micro or spot bonuses can reward specific extraordinary actions like passing a first-time inspection, going above and beyond for a client, or bringing in a referral.
teachings.quote: I'm actually evaluating, paying attention to micro bonuses because I want people to think I want them to be there for their team.

teachings.concept: Delivering on Time and on Budget is Not Bonus-Worthy
teachings.explanation: Meeting basic project expectations is the job, not an exceptional achievement deserving a bonus. Bonuses should reward outcomes that go beyond standard performance, such as client referrals or exceptional service moments.
teachings.quote: If you deliver a project on time and on budget, we should be bonused. And I said, I don't think so. That's just your job.

teachings.concept: 30-40% Pre-Construction Agreement Close Rate Signals Correct Pricing
teachings.explanation: A close rate of 30 to 40 percent on pre-construction agreements indicates pricing is appropriately set. A much higher rate suggests underpricing, while a very low rate may indicate the market or messaging needs adjustment.
teachings.quote: 30 to 40% means you're priced right. If you're 70 or 80%, you're not priced correctly.

teachings.concept: Steady 20% Annual Net Profit Growth as a Scaling Benchmark
teachings.explanation: For custom home builders in the long game, aiming for 20% annual net profit growth is a realistic and sustainable scaling model. Rapid growth or aggressive owner bonusing can destabilize a business.
teachings.quote: Your net profit wants to grow 20% a year. Like to me, I would look at big picture. Like it wants to grow 20% a year.

teachings.concept: Client Buy-In as a Prerequisite to Project Success
teachings.explanation: Getting clients to actively participate in and agree to the pricing and selection process prevents surprises and disputes later. Drawing a parallel to medicine, the goal is to get clients to commit to the process so the project can succeed.
teachings.quote: Her only job is to get them to buy into her recipe so they can live. Like that is what she's masterful in. And I've learned a lot from her, but like, I think there's something wise there to just like, we got to get people to buy into the system.

actionable_advice: Develop detailed checklists for pre-construction scope reviews by trade (e.g., 20 yes/no questions on roofing and gutters) so that team members can conduct thorough reviews without requiring owner presence at every meeting.
Assign the project manager who will own the project during construction to attend pre-construction pricing meetings so they understand the budget and prevent costly miscommunications at handoff.
Hold bi-weekly OAC meetings during pre-construction, mirroring the structure used during construction, to build team chemistry and allow owner-level check-ins at a high level.
Structure employee bonuses around company-wide net profit rather than individual project profit to encourage collaboration and avoid internal competition among project managers.
Give a spot bonus of $25,000 to any team member who refers a new project that the company takes on, as a referral incentive.
Use a tiered pricing guide (good, better, best) with pictures for each finish category so clients can visually understand trade-offs and actively participate in budget decisions.
Present clients with a running visual tracker of scope additions made during design so they can see exactly how their choices have added cost, making budget conversations less surprising.
Structure pre-construction fees with a credit toward the build if the client moves forward, and include a non-refundable clause if they do not, to protect the investment of time.

New business owners and early-stage companies should focus on building a strong culture and offering growth opportunities before formalizing bonus structures, rather than leading with monetary incentives.
Use spec homes strategically as marketing tools by hosting community events such as art shows or local group gatherings in the completed home to build relationships and attract custom home clients.
client_challenges: How to structure a small construction team so that estimating and project management responsibilities are clearly owned without creating gaps at the handoff from pre-construction to production.
How to incentivize employees fairly without creating animosity between project managers or rewarding people simply for doing their baseline job.
How to determine whether monthly operating expenditures of approximately $22,000 are healthy for a company doing $6 to $10 million in revenue, and what benchmarks to use.
How to deal with project start delays caused by high-net-worth clients who take their time in the sales process, resulting in poor cash flow in early months of the year.
How to handle design-build clients who continuously add scope during the design phase, driving the budget beyond what they originally said they were comfortable with.

How to price pre-construction services (flat rate vs. hourly vs. deliverable-based) and what to do when clients do not understand why they should pay for pre-construction separately from construction.
How to politely decline projects that are not a good fit and be more selective about which opportunities to pursue.
How to transition from spec home building into custom home building and what pitfalls to avoid when building out a team and profit-sharing structure for a relatively new company.
notable_quotes: On estimating: 'The real fast and easy answer is it's arduous. I spent six hours yesterday that I did not want to spend on looking at pricing for a project. So to me, it's never been a smooth operation period.'
On pre-construction close rates: '30 to 40% means you're priced right. If you're 70 or 80%, you're not priced correctly.'
On bonusing for basic performance: 'If you deliver a project on time and on budget, we should be bonused. And I said, I don't think so. That's just your job.'
On long-term scaling: 'A lot of businesses go under because they hire too fast and, or the owner's bonusing the heck out of themselves and there's nothing left.'
On client buy-in: 'Her only job is to get them to buy into her recipe so they can live. I think there's something wise there — we got to get people to buy into the system.'

On culture before compensation: 'For the first probably 10, 12 years, there were no bonuses. It was like, get it done, get it done. Let's get it done together. I'll do everything I can to help you grow. There's a culture that needs to be developed.'
